summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software developers should ship version 1 of their product even when it falls short of their vision, rather than delaying to achieve perfection. He acknowledges the many ways projects go wrong—schedule pressure, technical compromises, team friction, misunderstood customers—but contends that delaying release to fix these issues is a bigger mistake than shipping flawed software. The key insight is that real-world user feedback is more valuable than additional development time spent in isolation. By shipping early, teams can redirect their efforts based on actual usage data rather than assumptions. What matters most is not the initial quality but the team's velocity and responsiveness to user feedback after release.
key_points: Every software project ends up as a 'pale shadow' of the original vision due to schedule pressure, technical compromises, and unforeseen problems
Delaying release to fix known issues means missing out on discovering the 'unknown unknowns' that only real users can reveal
Three months of isolated polishing is less valuable than three months of iterating based on real user feedback
Team responsiveness and iteration velocity matter more than any single release's quality
Optimizing for near-perfect releases means optimizing for the wrong thing
money_quotes: At the end of the development cycle, you end up with software that is a pale shadow of the shining, glorious monument to software engineering that you envisioned when you started.
Yes, you did a ton of things wrong on this project. But you also did a ton of things wrong that you don't know about yet.

Instead of spending three months fixing up this version in a sterile, isolated lab, you could be spending that same three month period listening to feedback from real live, honest-to-god, annoying dedicated users of your software.
The velocity and responsiveness of your team to user feedback will set the tone for your software, far more than any single release ever could.
If you aren't embarrassed by v1.0 you didn't release it early enough.
To the extent that you're optimizing for near-perfect software releases, you're optimizing for the wrong thing.
Even if version 1 sucks, ship it anyway.
key_insight: Real-world user feedback is more valuable than any amount of isolated development time, so ship early and iterate fast rather than pursuing perfection before release.
